The **Progressive Conservative Party of Saskatchewan** is a [conservative](/wiki/Canadian_conservatism "Canadian conservatism") [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") in the [Canadian province](/wiki/Canadian_province "Canadian province") of [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"). Founded in 1905 by former [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories") Premier [Frederick Haultain](/wiki/Frederick_Haultain "Frederick Haultain"), the party was first known as the Provincial Rights Party. In 1912, its name changed to the Conservative Party of Saskatchewan, and in 1942 it adopted its current name. Members are commonly known as [Tories](/wiki/Tory_(disambiguation) "Tory (disambiguation)").

The party has formed government in Saskatchewan three times, first in a coalition government from 1929 to 1934, and then in consecutive majority governments from 1982 to 1991. The party was badly damaged by an expense fraud scandal in the 1990s. In 1997, the party went dormant when much of its membership migrated to the newly established [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party"). Although the party has been active again since the 2007 provincial election, no PC candidates have been elected since 1995.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years (1905–1934)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years (1905–1934)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/93/FrederickWAGHaultain.png)](/wiki/File:FrederickWAGHaultain.png)

A portrait of the party's first leader, [Frederick Haultain](/wiki/Frederick_Haultain "Frederick Haultain").

The party was founded in 1905. Saskatchewan joined Confederation on September 1, 1905, and its first election was slated for December of that year. Former Northwest Territories Premier Frederick Haultain had been instrumental in the granting of provincehood. However, while he had advocated for the creation of one large western province—for which he favoured the name [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo_(province) "Buffalo (province)")—[Wilfrid Laurier](/wiki/Wilfrid_Laurier "Wilfrid Laurier")'s federal government opted to create two new provinces instead, with [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") joining Confederation alongside Saskatchewan. Moreover, like [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba") a generation earlier, the two new provinces were denied rights over their natural resources.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In response, Haultain re-branded his [Territorial Conservative Association](/wiki/North-West_Territories_Liberal-Conservative_Party "North-West Territories Liberal-Conservative Party") as the Provincial Rights Party in a bid to become premier of Saskatchewan.[[3]](#cite_note-CPS-3)

Haultain's party was defeated by the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Liberal Party of Saskatchewan") in the [1905 election](/wiki/1905_Saskatchewan_general_election "1905 Saskatchewan general election"), becoming the Official Opposition with 47.5% of the vote. The [1908 election](/wiki/1908_Saskatchewan_general_election "1908 Saskatchewan general election") brought a similar result, with the PR party remaining in Opposition. In 1912, Haultain left politics when he was appointed the Chief Justice of Saskatchewan.[[4]](#cite_note-4) When he left, the party opted to re-adopt the Conservative name under the leadership of [Wellington Willoughby](/wiki/Wellington_Willoughby "Wellington Willoughby"). The change did not improve the party's fortunes; it lost a third straight [election in 1912](/wiki/1912_Saskatchewan_general_election "1912 Saskatchewan general election") to the Liberals and Premier [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott_(Canadian_politician) "Walter Scott (Canadian politician)"), with its share of the vote falling to 42%. In fact, the Liberals built an impressive political dynasty, securing further majority governments in 1917, 1921, and 1925. In 1921, the Tories were reduced to third-party status as the agrarian [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Progressive Party of Saskatchewan") gained Opposition status. However, party fortunes began to improve when [James T. M. Anderson](/wiki/James_T._M._Anderson "James T. M. Anderson") became leader in 1924.

Anderson had moved to Saskatchewan in 1908 to be a teacher, and in 1918 the Liberal government appointed him as provincial director of education among new Canadians, a role in which he advocated for more [assimilationist](/wiki/Assimilationist "Assimilationist") policies towards the rapidly growing immigrant population.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The following decade, Anderson capitalized on the racial and religious animosity created by the rise of the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan_in_Canada "Ku Klux Klan in Canada") in Saskatchewan—the organization boasted 25,000 members in the province by 1929—to gain support for Conservative policies on immigration and education;[[3]](#cite_note-CPS-3)[[6]](#cite_note-6) in 1928, Liberal Premier [Jimmy Gardiner](/wiki/James_Garfield_Gardiner "James Garfield Gardiner") claimed that the Klan was a tool of the Conservative Party.[[7]](#cite_note-Klan-7) There certainly was crossover between the organizations, and the Conservatives aided Klan organization by providing it with party membership lists.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In the [1929 election](/wiki/1929_Saskatchewan_general_election "1929 Saskatchewan general election"), Anderson led the party to its best performance in the first half of the twentieth century, securing 36% of the popular vote and 24 of 63 seats. Despite having fewer seats than the Liberals' 28, the Conservatives were able to form a [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") with the support of Progressive and Independents Legislative members (MLAs), and Anderson became [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan").[[9]](#cite_note-9) What was known as the "Co-operative government" would introduce amendments to the *Schools Act*, banning [French](/wiki/Canadian_French "Canadian French") as a language of instruction and the display of religious symbols in [Catholic schools](/wiki/Separate_school "Separate school"), amendments applauded at the 1930 Klan convention.[[7]](#cite_note-Klan-7) The government was also successful in curbing patronage through its Public Service Act.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The Anderson-led coalition took office immediately before the onset of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Canada "Great Depression in Canada"), which proved particularly severe on the Prairies. The government's retrenchment in response—similar to [R. B. Bennett](/wiki/R._B._Bennett "R. B. Bennett")'s federal [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada_(1867%E2%80%931942) "Conservative Party of Canada (1867–1942)") government—opened it to attacks from the Liberals as well as a new socialist political movement going by the name of Farmer–Labour, which would in 1935 become the [Co-operative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Saskatchewan Co-operative Commonwealth Federation") (CCF).[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The government did establish the Saskatchewan Relief Commission to provide some aid to residents, but this did not address the systemic issues creating depression conditions. In 1932, Anderson asked Liberal leader Gardiner to join the coalition, but the Opposition refused.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In the [1934 election](/wiki/1934_Saskatchewan_general_election "1934 Saskatchewan general election"), the Liberals returned to power and Anderson's Conservatives were wiped out, failing to win a single seat as the Farmer–Labour Group took over Official Opposition status.

### Political wilderness (1934–1975)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Political wilderness (1934–1975)")]

After the 1934 defeat, Anderson stayed on as party leader until 1936, when the party chose a young lawyer, [John Diefenbaker](/wiki/John_Diefenbaker "John Diefenbaker"), as his successor. Although Diefenbaker would go on to revive the fortunes of the federal [Progressive Conservatives](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Progressive Conservative Party of Canada") in the 1950s, he was not able to gain ground as the provincial party leader. However, it was not just Diefenbaker. Between 1934 and 1975, only two PC members—the party adopted the Progressive Conservative name in 1942, in line with the federal party—would be elected to the Saskatchewan Legislature: one in a 1953 by-election, and party leader [Martin Pederson](/wiki/Martin_Pederson "Martin Pederson") in the [1964 election](/wiki/1964_Saskatchewan_general_election "1964 Saskatchewan general election"); neither was re-elected.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Those years were marked by an effective return to a two-party system consisting of the Liberals and the CCF, the latter of which formed five successive majority governments from 1944 to 1964, after which it became the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") (NDP). The PCs spent long periods without a leader, and in the early 1970s nearly merged with the provincial [Social Credit](/wiki/Social_Credit_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Social Credit Party of Saskatchewan") party;[[15]](#cite_note-:0-15) PC members in fact approved the merger in November 1970, but it was ultimately rejected by Social Credit members in February 1971.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Return to the Legislature (1975–1982)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Return to the Legislature (1975–1982)")]

The PC party was rejuvenated by the leadership of [Dick Collver](/wiki/Dick_Collver "Dick Collver"), who took over the party reins in 1973.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The Liberals had formed two consecutive majority governments from 1964 to 1971; however, they were soundly defeated by [Allan Blakeney](/wiki/Allan_Blakeney "Allan Blakeney")'s NDP in 1971, and Liberal leader [Ross Thatcher](/wiki/Ross_Thatcher "Ross Thatcher") died just weeks after the election. Encouraged by the popularity of the federal PCs in Saskatchewan, Collver sensed an opportunity to gain ground, and presented himself as a populist leader railing against the big government of the NDP.[[15]](#cite_note-:0-15) The Tories returned to the legislature in the [1975 election](/wiki/1975_Saskatchewan_general_election "1975 Saskatchewan general election"), winning 7 seats to the Liberals' 15 and the NDP's 39, marking the first time since 1929 that three major parties had sizeable representation in the Legislature. The results were even better for the Tories in the [1978 election](/wiki/1978_Saskatchewan_general_election "1978 Saskatchewan general election") as they surged to Official Opposition status, winning 17 seats while the Liberals were shut out for the first time in provincial history.

In a twist, Collver resigned the party leadership in 1979 to sit as an Independent. Some within the party had begun to see Collver and his business dealings—he was embroiled in a lawsuit with the provincial Government Insurance Office—as a liability.[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18) As an Independent, he still focused his criticism on the NDP government.[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18) Then, in March 1980, he announced the formation of a new party, which he called the [Unionest Party](/wiki/Unionest_Party "Unionest Party").[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) Apparently seized by prevalent feelings of [western alienation](/wiki/Western_alienation "Western alienation") and anti-communism, Collver's new party advocated for a secession of Saskatchewan and the other western provinces from Canada and a union with the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")—the name Unionest was a portmanteau of "best" and "union".[[20]](#cite_note-20) One other PC MLA, [Dennis Ham](/wiki/Dennis_Ham "Dennis Ham"), joined Collver in sitting as a Unionest, but neither ran for re-election in 1982 and the party was dissolved.[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18)[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) Collver's abrupt departure from the PCs led to agricultural economist [Grant Devine](/wiki/Grant_Devine "Grant Devine") being named leader. Devine had lost his election bid in 1978, and he lost again in a 1980 by-election in the [Estevan](/wiki/Estevan_(electoral_district) "Estevan (electoral district)") constituency. However, he stayed at the helm, leading the party into the [1982 election](/wiki/1982_Saskatchewan_general_election "1982 Saskatchewan general election").

### Devine government (1982–1991)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Devine government (1982–1991)")]

Devine led the Progressive Conservatives to a [majority government](/wiki/Majority_government "Majority government") for the first time in 1982, stunning the NDP by winning 55 out of 64 seats, reducing Blakeney's party to nine seats. Devine had run on a program of tax and mortgage relief and on promoting free enterprise under the slogan, "There's so much more we can be". Notably, not a single member of the PC caucus had experience in government, and observers have noted that during this term, Devine's government "lacked ideological clarity".[[21]](#cite_note-21) The PCs did cut taxes—eliminating the province's 20% gasoline tax—and royalties, but it kept most government assets public and spent freely, rapidly running up a string of deficits. It used public money to support private ventures and, more than anything else, agriculture.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23) The government offered a universal, low-interest loan program to farmers that cost the province $1 billion.[[24]](#cite_note-24) By 1986, the government had raised the provincial debt to $1.5 billion. Part of Devine's strategy was a close relationship with [Brian Mulroney](/wiki/Brian_Mulroney "Brian Mulroney")'s federal PC government, and in a particularly important move, Devine secured a $1 billion farming aid package from Ottawa on the eve of the [1986 election](/wiki/1986_Saskatchewan_general_election "1986 Saskatchewan general election").[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23)

Saskatchewan historian [Bill Waiser](/wiki/Bill_Waiser "Bill Waiser") has argued that the Devine years marked a fundamental shift in Saskatchewan politics, with a widening divide between urban and rural Saskatchewan re-defining the province's political landscape.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Similarly, political analyst Dale Eisler has written that Saskatchewan politics can be grouped into distinct "Before Devine" and "After Devine" categories.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Devine's intent focus on rural priorities, and farming in particular, contributed to this divide. The results of the 1986 election bear this out. Blakeney's NDP narrowly edged out the PCs in the popular vote, but the concentration of that vote in urban centres—the NDP won 16 out of 20 seats in Regina and Saskatoon—allowed Devine's PC, who won a majority of rural seats, to form another majority government. In effect, Devine staked the PCs on rural support.

Possibly owing in part to the divided election result, and doubtless owing in part to a severe downturn in the provincial economy, Devine's government adopted a more distinct ideological approach in its second term, which observers have labelled a "new right" neoliberal approach of cuts to spending and substantial privatization.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The government sold off major assets such as [SaskOil](/wiki/SaskOil "SaskOil") and [PotashCorp](/wiki/PotashCorp "PotashCorp"), which had been significant sources of revenue and key achievements of the previous NDP government. The PCs also attempted to privatize [SaskEnergy](/wiki/SaskEnergy "SaskEnergy"), but a legislative walk-out by the Opposition—and a public petition with over 100,000 signatures—prevented it.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) During this term, the government was also criticized for undemocratic legislative tactics, including proroguing the Legislature and financing expenditures through the dubious use of special warrants.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) Outrage toward its conduct and agenda—the government's cuts to social spending precipitated what was one of the largest public protests in Regina history with approximately seven thousand people descending on the Legislature in 1987—along with the unpopularity of Mulroney's federal government and a growing fiscal crisis, which saw provincial debt soar to $13 billion by 1992, all led to Devine's government being defeated by the NDP in the [1991 election](/wiki/1991_Saskatchewan_general_election "1991 Saskatchewan general election").[[33]](#cite_note-33) By then, the fiscal crisis was so severe that the province was facing the risk of bankruptcy.[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

#### Thatcher murder conviction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Thatcher murder conviction")]

In January 1983, energy minister [Colin Thatcher](/wiki/Colin_Thatcher "Colin Thatcher") resigned from cabinet. Days after Thatcher's resignation, his ex-wife JoAnn Wilson was murdered, and Thatcher was charged with the crime in May 1984. In November 1984, a jury found him guilty and Thatcher was sentenced to life in prison for 25 years.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

#### Expense fraud scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Expense fraud scandal")]

In April 1995, after an [RCMP](/wiki/RCMP "RCMP") investigation dubbed "Project Fiddle", police charged a dozen current and former PC MLAs as well as two caucus workers with fraud relating to an expense fraud scheme that ran from 1987 to 1991.[[36]](#cite_note-:4-36) In 1987, the caucus agreed to pool a portion of communications allowances into a central account. The party's director of communications, John Scraba, siphoned cash from this account and stashed it in [safety deposit boxes](/wiki/Safety_deposit_box "Safety deposit box").[[36]](#cite_note-:4-36) Another former staffer alleged that this was common knowledge, and that "if you needed money, all you had to do was go to John Scraba".[[37]](#cite_note-:5-37) Instances included former cabinet minister [Joan Duncan](/wiki/Joan_Duncan "Joan Duncan"), who pleaded guilty to fraud in the amount of $12,405, which was spent on a vacation in [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"), and former MLA [Gerald Muirhead](/wiki/Gerald_Muirhead "Gerald Muirhead"), who was found guilty of spending over $2,000 on an embroidered saddle and bridle sash.[[36]](#cite_note-:4-36)

Money was distributed through invoices from a series of shell companies for services that were never rendered or expenses that were illegitimate.[[37]](#cite_note-:5-37) Overall, the fraudulent expense claims totalled approximately $837,000. Police were first alerted to the scheme in 1991 when a legislative clerk reported suspicious invoices.[[36]](#cite_note-:4-36) In 1992, bank officials drilled into an unclaimed safety deposit box that contained $150,000; the box was addressed to the PC caucus office in the Saskatchewan Legislature.[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23) Nineteen staff or MLAs were charged in the scheme, and fifteen were convicted—including ten cabinet members and a caucus chair—with many serving jail time.[[38]](#cite_note-:7-38) Scraba was sentenced to two years in prison. Former MLA [Michael Hopfner](/wiki/Michael_Hopfner "Michael Hopfner"), whose defence claimed he was too financially incompetent to commit fraud, was sentenced to 18 months. The stiffest sentence went to former caucus chair [Lorne McLaren](/wiki/Lorne_McLaren "Lorne McLaren") at three and a half years.[[37]](#cite_note-:5-37) Other party members convicted in the scandal included [Robert Andrew](/wiki/Robert_Lynal_Andrew "Robert Lynal Andrew"), [Harry Baker](/wiki/Harry_Daniel_Baker "Harry Daniel Baker"), [Eric Bernston](/wiki/Eric_Berntson "Eric Berntson"), [John Gerich](/wiki/John_Gerich "John Gerich"), [Grant Hodgins](/wiki/Grant_Hodgins "Grant Hodgins"), [Harold Martens](/wiki/Harold_Martens "Harold Martens"), [Beattie Martin](/wiki/Gordon_Beattie_Martin "Gordon Beattie Martin"), Michael McCafferty, [Ray Meiklejohn](/wiki/Ray_Meiklejohn "Ray Meiklejohn"), and [Sherwin Petersen](/wiki/Sherwin_Petersen "Sherwin Petersen").[[38]](#cite_note-:7-38)

Former cabinet minister [Jack Wolfe](/wiki/John_Thomas_Wolfe "John Thomas Wolfe") died by suicide in 1995. Already faced with the prospect of having to testify against former colleagues, Wolfe was informed by his lawyer on 2 February that he was going to be arrested; he was found dead that day.[[37]](#cite_note-:5-37)

Grant Devine was never charged in the fraud scheme; he testified that he was too busy to pay attention to finances and was unaware of the scheme, although he took responsibility as the leader of the party.[[36]](#cite_note-:4-36)

### Opposition, the Sask Party, and dormancy (1991–2005)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Opposition, the Sask Party, and dormancy (1991–2005)")]

The PCs were reduced to 10 seats in 1991 and became the Official Opposition. Devine resigned as PC leader in 1992, and [Rick Swenson](/wiki/Rick_Swenson_(politician) "Rick Swenson (politician)")—who, like Devine, was not implicated in the expense fraud scandal—became interim leader. In 1994, the party chose [Bill Boyd](/wiki/Bill_Boyd_(Canadian_politician) "Bill Boyd (Canadian politician)") as its new permanent leader. Particularly when news began to break about the expense fraud scandal, the PCs were expected to be wiped out in the [1995 election](/wiki/1995_Saskatchewan_general_election "1995 Saskatchewan general election"). Boyd attempted to promote the party as the "New PCs", while some PC candidates left the party name off their campaign materials altogether.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The PCs ultimately held on to five seats as the Liberals surpassed them to become the Opposition to [Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow")'s NDP. Romanow had pursued further spending cuts in order to address the province's fiscal crisis. While the NDP had balanced the finances by 1995, extensive cuts to rural health and education services further exacerbated the urban-rural divide.[[23]](#cite_note-:3-23)[[40]](#cite_note-40) With neither the PCs nor the Liberals seeing a clear path back to power, four MLAs from each party—including Boyd and all representing rural districts—agreed in 1997 to walk away from their parties and help establish a new conservative party in a bid to unite the opposition to the NDP.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") was officially registered on 17 September 1997, and with eight MLAs, it became the Official Opposition.

Most PC members migrated to the new party—Romanow liked to emphasize this by calling the new party the "Saskatories"—and the party executive voted to effectively put the scandal-plagued PC Party into dormancy for at least two election cycles.[[42]](#cite_note-42) In order to remain registered, the party ran a handful of [paper candidates](/wiki/Paper_candidate "Paper candidate") in both the 1999 and 2003 provincial elections. However, with so much of the PC membership along with its office and executive staff moving to the new party, the perception that the Sask Party was merely a re-branded PC Party persisted for years.[[43]](#cite_note-:6-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) Even Devine publicly expressed support for the Saskatchewan Party.[[43]](#cite_note-:6-43) One member who tried but was unable to make the move to the new party was PC MLA [Jack Gooshen](/wiki/Jack_Goohsen "Jack Goohsen"), who was convicted in 1999 of consorting with a 14-year old prostitute.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Recent history (2005–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Recent history (2005–present)")]

In June 2005, the dormant PCs announced that they were taking applications for new members, and that a members' meeting would be held to decide the future of the party.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Changes to provincial electoral laws passed during the previous Legislature decreased the number of candidates a party needed to run in general elections from ten to two. In 2006, members voted to resurrect the party and chose former interim leader Swenson as its new permanent leader.

The party ran into difficulties when trying to access its trust fund, which reportedly contained close to $3 million. In 2007, the party sued the Saskatchewan Party over access to the fund, alleging that the fund's trustees—three of whom were active in the Sask Party—were conspiring with the Sask Party to deny them access and halt the PC party revival.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The case was not settled until 2014, when Swenson announced that the PCs had been given control of the fund.[[49]](#cite_note-49) In the meantime, the party ran five candidates in each of the [2007](/wiki/2007_Saskatchewan_general_election "2007 Saskatchewan general election") and [2011](/wiki/2011_Saskatchewan_general_election "2011 Saskatchewan general election") elections, capturing less than 0.5% of the overall vote in both elections. The party ran 18 candidates in the [2016 election](/wiki/2016_Saskatchewan_general_election "2016 Saskatchewan general election"), its largest number since 1995, and collected 1.3% of the vote while finishing third in 10 districts.

Swenson announced after the 2016 election that he would step aside as leader once a new leader was chosen.[[50]](#cite_note-50) On November 3, 2018, Ken Grey was elected as the new leader of the party.[[51]](#cite_note-51) In the [2020 provincial election](/wiki/2020_Saskatchewan_general_election "2020 Saskatchewan general election"), the party ran 31 candidates and captured 1.9% of the vote.

Grey resigned as leader on January 18, 2021. Later in the year, Grey alleged that [conspiracy theorists](/wiki/Conspiracy_theorists "Conspiracy theorists") and [far-right](/wiki/Far-right "Far-right") elements were working to take over the party, a charge that the party denied.[[1]](#cite_note-StarPhoenix-1) Grey cited as an example that he had been "inundated" with [Islamophobic](/wiki/Islamophobic "Islamophobic") messages after he was photographed with an official from [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") in 2019.[[1]](#cite_note-StarPhoenix-1) In November 2022, Rose Buscholl was appointed as interim leader in charge of a three-point revitalization plan, including building constituency organizations, engaging with members, and developing new policy.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Party insiders also stated that a name change was likely for the party, since the 'progressive' term had been "usurped by liberal socialists".[[1]](#cite_note-StarPhoenix-1)

In 2022, the party trust fund would again be at the centre of controversy. The party alleged that a group of political organizers had attempted a hostile takeover of the PCs, with a plan to rename the party the Saskatchewan Conservative Party and to install former Sask Party MLA [Nadine Wilson](/wiki/Nadine_Wilson "Nadine Wilson") as leader.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) The PCs reported that they rejected the attempted takeover, and that Wilson and the other organizers instead formed the [Saskatchewan United Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_United_Party "Saskatchewan United Party"). The PCs further alleged that Sask United organizers stole a hard drive containing member and donor information, a charge United organizers denied.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In 2023, Buscholl became the permanent leader of the party, removing the interim tag.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Ahead of the [2024 provincial election](/wiki/2024_Saskatchewan_general_election "2024 Saskatchewan general election"), Buscholl approached the far-right [Buffalo Party](/wiki/Buffalo_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Buffalo Party of Saskatchewan") to discuss a merger. On September 12, Buscholl announced that the PCs were calling off the merger discussions, citing Buffalo's "extensive focus on out-of-scope federal and international issues" as a barrier.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The party ultimately finished in fifth place for the fifth consecutive election, receiving approximately one percent of the vote province wide.[[58]](#cite_note-58)
